architecture.summary: This is a theoretical paper formalizing causal abstraction as a mathematical framework for mechanistic interpretability of neural networks. It does not present a novel neural architecture or training method. Instead, it provides formal definitions and proofs connecting high-level causal models (e.g., symbolic algorithms) to low-level causal models (e.g., neural networks) through alignments between variables, causal consistency conditions, and interchange interventions. Information flows conceptually: a researcher specifies an alignment between clusters of low-level variables and high-level variables; causal abstraction then validates whether interventions on the low-level model produce results consistent with the high-level model's causal laws.

biblio.title: Causal Abstraction: A Theoretical Foundation for Mechanistic Interpretability

claims.statement: Causal abstraction provides a mathematical framework for mechanistic interpretability by defining when a human-intelligible, high-level causal explanation is a faithful interpretation of opaque low-level details.
We generalize causal abstraction to cyclic causal structures and typed high-level variables, extending beyond prior work limited to acyclic models.

We prove that constructive abstraction holds exactly when the high-level model can be constructed from the low-level model by marginalizing away variables, merging sets of variables, and merging values of variables.
A notion of approximate causal abstraction allows assessment of the degree to which a high-level causal model is a causal abstraction of a lower-level one, defining graded faithfulness.

Multi-source interchange interventions can be used to conduct causal abstraction analyses for high-level causal models with multiple intermediate variables, generalizing prior work restricted to single intermediate variables.

The methods LIME, causal effect estimation, causal mediation analysis, iterated nullspace projection, and circuit-based explanations are all formalizable as special cases of causal abstraction analysis.
Integrated gradients can be used to compute interchange interventions and conduct causal abstraction analysis.
